item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great article, but - that hebrew text in the afterward is complete gibberish. What's it supposed to mean?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one single article has resulted in one fantastic evening reading Sarkic SCPs and tales and I thank you for both that and this SCP on its own. Great read, 10/10

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like when authors take "bad" GoI's or concepts and show their kind side, it doesn't make sense in many cases, but here you make us realize that Sarkicism is a huge thing and, as all huge things, has many separate parts that sometimes stray from the main course or go against the grain. It's wordy, I'll give you that, and filled to the brim with (sometimes superfluous) footnotes. But it also takes risks and explains the world in a really cool way. Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article was, curiously, the first skip I read on Sarkicism. And I just love it.

You really have a problem with length. I'm not too different on this, but I once heard something about chinese traditional painting that stuck with me: the beauty is not in the lines, but in the voids.

I found it curious that most people who criticized this skip made two complains besides the length, that it is cliché and that it isn't horrific. What I do find so enjoyable about this, and they seemed to not see, is that it is unique precisely for not being horrific. This contributed more than anything else to make the Sarkic religion seems like something believable.

Also, they are have a striking deviation from other Sarkic communities: they are not isolationists. Just think about it: 1/5th of the village aren't Sarkicists, and they engage in trade with the world around them. There was no reference to technophobia, either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I approve of all your proposed edits. Make it so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk Team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This is one doozy of a skip, with a ton of references and connections to other articles on the site. There's a lot that can be done with it:

Special Containment Procedures: SCP-2815 is to be monitored on a 24-hour basis by members of MTF Epsilon-6 “Village Idiots”1.

Epsilon-6 has a blurb on the Mobile Task Forces page, and can be linked there:

Special Containment Procedures: SCP-2815 is to be monitored on a 24-hour basis by members of MTF Epsilon-6 “Village Idiots”2.

This task is delegated to MTF Psi-13 "Witch Hunters"3, due to their infiltrators already being established within GOI-0385 for surveillance.

The Witch Hunters first appeared in SCP-2408, and this mention can be linked to that skip.

This task is delegated to MTF Psi-13 "Witch Hunters"4, due to their infiltrators already being established within GOI-0385 for surveillance.

It is assumed that the N,N-DMT serves a similar purpose as it has in SCP-2480: revealing anomalously hidden features that are invisible to the naked eye or mundane recording equipment.

This explicit mention of 2480 doesn't currently have a link, one can be added.

It is assumed that the N,N-DMT serves a similar purpose as it has in SCP-2480: revealing anomalously hidden features that are invisible to the naked eye or mundane recording equipment.

Though outwardly portraying themselves as moderately devout Roman Catholics, investigations have revealed the community to be practising a religion derived from Hungarian paganism displaying a high degree of syncretism with a heterodox variety of Sarkicism

This is a good place to link to the Sarkicism hub.

Though outwardly portraying themselves as moderately devout Roman Catholics, investigations have revealed the community to be practising a religion derived from Hungarian paganism displaying a high degree of syncretism with a heterodox variety of Sarkicism

Trust me when I say that I'm just as appalled at this decision as you are. Should you feel that your current posting won't allow you to make a difference, send me a signal. The Abyss Gazers5

This can link to another SCP that has the Abyss Gazers, I recommend 3989.

Trust me when I say that I'm just as appalled at this decision as you are. Should you feel that your current posting won't allow you to make a difference, send me a signal. The Abyss Gazers6

If anyone has any other ideas, feel free to suggest them below.

Footnotes
1. Mobile Task Force Epsilon-6 specializes in the investigation, containment, and subsequent cleanup of anomalies in rural and suburban environments.
2. Mobile Task Force Epsilon-6 specializes in the investigation, containment, and subsequent cleanup of anomalies in rural and suburban environments.
3. Mobile Task Force Psi-13 is a highly classified joint Foundation/GOC task force designed for the infiltration of Sarkic organizations and the termination of high threat members. Operatives are trained in Counter Occult Stratagems (COS) and the use of corrosive/incendiary armaments.
4. Mobile Task Force Psi-13 is a highly classified joint Foundation/GOC task force designed for the infiltration of Sarkic organizations and the termination of high threat members. Operatives are trained in Counter Occult Stratagems (COS) and the use of corrosive/incendiary armaments.
5. Mobile Task Force Psi-9 ("Abyss Gazers") is a battalion-strength force, consisting of personnel from the Foundation and Global Occult Coalition, trained in unconventional warfare against invading enemies through the use of heavy artillery, DMT enhanced perception, and Counter Occult Stratagems (COS).
6. Mobile Task Force Psi-9 ("Abyss Gazers") is a battalion-strength force, consisting of personnel from the Foundation and Global Occult Coalition, trained in unconventional warfare against invading enemies through the use of heavy artillery, DMT enhanced perception, and Counter Occult Stratagems (COS).

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that I've shrunk down one of the images to 10%, so it's not as enormous and won't take awhile to load. The original file remains localized. Author, you may make further changes as you see necessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Though I'd finally reply to these critiques. Better late than never and all that.

  1. Length: I kind of expected this to be a long skip (because I like those), though never quite this long. Some of the additions I did to add more closure to the article, per reader request, others to clarify one point or another, also by user request. Keeping things brief has always been a problem for me, as I often end up being really verbose due to a nigh on obsessive compulsion to cover every little detail. I'm working on that, and the other skips I'm working on less than a third as long as this. Partly because I also want to challenge myself.
  2. Repitition: The footnotes and explanations of concepts already mentioned in the hub are there mostly first-time or new readers, and those otherwise unfamiliar with Sarkicism as a whole. It's also there to build a sense of interconnectivity, to show that 2815 is but a smaller piece in a greater mosaic. I've always loved that in other skips, as it gives the illusion that there's a lot more in the text than what the text describes itself.
  3. Unoriginality: I can see the point about the secluded Sarkic village. I suppose that's sort of become a tired trope with Sarkicism in general, and I can't but admit to have done exactly that. However, I'd like to emphasize that the culture described is also largely benign and non-hostile, which I can't remember seeing in other Sarkic material (there's always a catch there). I wanted to write something that depicted Sarkites as normal human beings (well, as normal as one can be for an anomalous community), because it's kind of hard to build a society, let alone an empire, around evil fleschcrafting. I also wanted to make an explicitly benign skip, which Metaphysician had wanted to do/see as well, so as to broaden the field on what Sarkic societies look like.
  4. Sarkicism: I suppose I can't blame you. I don't know whether there was a huge wave of Sarkic articles before I posted this (but judging from the reactions, it seems to have been the case), since I only joined in September 2016. At that point, I was absolutely crazy for Sarkicism, and it's still one of my favourite GoI/canons, which is why I wanted to write something about that specifically. Also, it gave me a good excuse to set the skip in Hungary. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Köszönöm! Nagyon örülök, hogy tetszik neked. :)

And I really appreciate the correction. Although I speak Hungarian natively, I grew up in Sweden and didn't have much contact with other Hungarian speakers outside my immediate family, except for summer breaks when we went to Budapest. A lot of my Hungarian is stuff I've learnt on the fly by trying to read newspapers and literature, and conversing with elderly people, so there are bound to be some mistakes. But any advice is appreciated, since it helps me get better at the language. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a Hungarian native and also a big fan of Sarkicism in general, this made me very, very happy. It's an expansive, well-built skip with a fair bit of great worldbuilding and connections made, even if sometimes it's a bit too long.

Just a small correction, if you'll accept it.
"Tudjuk, hogy közeledik mennyországunk,
Amit csakis saját húsunkból megszülhetjük. szülhetünk meg"

If you're not Hungarian, very well researched; if you are, then gratulálok, ez nagyon jó lett. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They kept it under wraps. As stated in the article, the SCP outwardly resembles a Roman Catholic community in Hungary. Pretty normal, by any standards. The only reason the Foundation got any concrete evidence on them is because of an agent they embedded into the community via marriage. Like any Sarkic cult, they are highly secretive in that sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They had famous anthropologists come into town and study them but they failed to notice the crazy flesh sorcerer cult?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the info! I had no idea about this case, nor did I know that such a process was considered rape. It puts some well-needed perspective on a lot of things.

Nevertheless, the morality of the procedure, while far from irrelevant in and of itself, probably matters less to the Foundation than securing intel on the SCP in question. The Foundation is, as I've come to understand, very much a "the ends justify the means" kind of organization, and judging from other skips and previous conduct, are not at all averse to subjecting people to horrendous conditions and treatment if they believe it necessary. Moreover, since the woman is labelled a skip by the Foundation, they would likely be even less concerned about her well-being. From what I know, the Foundation doesn't grant any general rights to skips, rather letting the Ethics Committee decide that on a case by case basis.

To clarify: This isn't an ethical statement on my part. I didn't write it because I considered it morally okay, by any means; even less so now. I do, however, keep it, because in my headcanon the Foundation is just that coldhearted when it feels it needs to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off, for the tl;dr crowd: This is a text based community. QED.

Second, the amount of effort put into the cross indexing by the author should be commended, not criticized.

Third, there is no Canon. Any conflict from ' out of character' behaviour is due to headcanon popping up, and that does not an proper argument make.

I am personally very impressed with both the original content, and the presentation. Thank you for contributing this detailed look into a SC p community. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like Sarkicism. So I wasn't planning to vote. Then I saw this.

Agent Balogh is to infiltrate SCP-2815 via initiating a romantic relationship with an instance of SCP-2815-1 (designated SCP-2815-1-A), with the aim of marrying into the family, producing offspring and (unless posing significant danger to containment) be officially inducted into the community.

No. Absolutely not. This is unacceptable. This is rape. This is a protracted, lifelong rape.

edit: read this

https://www.theguardian.com/commentisfree/2013/jun/28/sexual-behaviour-undercover-police

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see your point. However, there is plenty of material to both suggest and show that the Foundation and the GOC actively cooperate. The GoI list itself says that the two cooperate from time to time, if I remember correctly. Moreover, MTF Psi-9 and Psi-13, as well as Sitra Achra, aren't mine inventions, but taken from existing articles dealing with Sarkicism. Psi-13 "Witch Hunters" already appears in SCP-2408, while Psi-9 "Abyss Gazers" appear in SCP-2408, SCP-2264 and SCP-2480. Sitra Achra, meanwhile, is referred to in pretty much every major Sarkic article, and is introduced and expounded in SCP-2480.

So what I've done is mostly reused those.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for pointing that out!

Aye, I'd be glad it if was. And I love flags! Though credit goes to SunnyClockwork for the symbol on it; that's purely his work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! Hearing that is what makes writing these feel worth it. I'm glad you like it. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're quite right, and so I've done just that. The exploration log is on its own, separate page now. Thanks for your feedback! :)

item.title: Exploration Log moved
item.content:encoded:
Since the majority of negative criticism on this article has been directed at its sheer length, I've followed the suggestions of some to move the exploration log to a separate page. A link to it can be found under the "Addendum-3" tab.

Looking closer at it, I realized that it didn't provide any significant new info on the SCP proper that wasn't provided elsewhere. For all those of you who have provided critique and feedback for this article: Thank you! You've helped make this article better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sarkicism

That was enough to send a chill up my spine just by seeing it. Nothing good ever comes up when those guys are involved.

My initial reaction was not incorrect. +1.

item.title: Not quite. . . ..
item.content:encoded:
MC&D and Prometheus Labs are different. Prometheus Labs had huge economic resources, kept themselves well-hidden, etc.

Marshall, Carter and Dark are a different story altogether. It says in the information on them on their hub-page that they hold a massive worldwide financial influence among the world's Elite. "With a glance, Marshall, Carter and Dark could level a city, bankrupt a country; with a single call, they could plunge the planet into a thermonuclear war." Taking MC&D on is an even bigger risk than trying to stop their deals. One also has to consider that they wouldn't actively sell anomalies which could cause K-Class scenarios, due again to their desire to maintain their business. Obviously, the end of the world (or human society) would not allow that.

In this skip though, anomalous wears are being produced in a villiage, then consumed by or transplanted into civilians throughout a large area of Eastern Europe. These are Sarkic wares by the way, so for all we know, as benign as this villiage may seem, they could perhaps be planting genetic ticking time-bombs in all who consume the food or receive the blood of organs. It's possible that now, all those civilians can be converted into Sarkic flesh-beasts remotely by the villagers. Or not. Perhaps the whole thing really is benign. But does it seem possible that the O5 Council would take a gamble on that? Their goal, from the beginning, has always been the containment of any and all anomalies, even those deemed "safe." They'd intercept the shipments en-route (by infiltrating the Manna Charitable Foundation appropriately) and replace them with non-anomalous items, while continuing to monitor the village and its products for research on Sarkicism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm reminded of the line "Organ harvesting too (you'd think these Sarkic types would have them growing from trees)" from SCP-2408.

This is a pretty cool skip about a benevolent (?) Sarkic community.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's precedent for it. The Foundation doesn't encourage anomalous products, but they seem to focus on the ones that become a problem rather than trying vainly to stop every single MC&D auction or hair-brained Prometheus Laboratories gizmo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept, but the one thing that bothers me here is that I don't think the foundation would allow anomalous products into the market, even if they were revealed to be harmless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm…

+ wot i think
get rid of this stuff

I was really hoping for this mélange of cool Sarkic stuff that I've seen before to turn out to be something greater than the sum of its parts. Colour me disappointed.

Intrigued enough by the world-building and vivid detail - which are nearly spot-on, by the way - to look past the more tired tropes (another rural cult of organ-harvesting teratoma-worshipping cannibal sorcerers, yay…) and appreciate the more original stuff (like remote-control eviscerated horses, right on), I soldiered on, expecting a strong pay-off at the end.

My first thought upon opening the final collapsible was: "My God, I don't want to read all of this."

The exploration log, while overall a standard (read: boring) extradimensional MTF romp, has its moments:

It looks like a pussy, Sir.

This was the best sentence in the entire article. Thanks for this.

The belated "twist" of this tale, that the cannibal sorcerer flesh-cult actually isn't evil but is just giving its flesh-goods away with no ulterior motive, felt like an anticlimax. I was expecting you to throw in another twist at the last moment and end on a more horrifying note, but… nothing of the sort. In my opinion, it was not enough to make all that reading worth my while - and the worst part is that you keep coming back to it. You even made a supplementary page simply to repeat what you'd already stated twice. Once is enough.

In short, this story looks good at first, but doesn't innovate or ever really go anywhere plot-wise. A reluctant downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There appears to be a minor inconsistency; Agent Balogh's partner has been referred to as both SCP-2815-1-A and SCP-2815-1A (unless I've missed something and these designations refer to separate subjects).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, you really took some brave decisións with this skip. I gotta say, some work for me; other don't. However, all in all, this article deserves credit for going his own way. I had a shitton of thigns to say in pro and con of this, but I'm too busy, so I'll keep this as a placeholder so I can post 'em later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ive never been too into sarkic stuff, but this is really really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have one really big problem with this.

("Abyss Gazers") is a battalion-strength force, consisting of personnel from the Foundation and Global Occult Coalition,

Psi-13 is a highly classified joint Foundation/GOC task force

At least, in my headcanon, there's simply no way a collaboration of this variety could come about. The GOC and Foundation hold many aesthetic similarities but their internal methodologies are diametrically opposed. One protects, the other destroys. And the reason they always get so pissy at one another is because both are really good at their jobs. If a joint operation of any sort is taking place, it's STRIKE and an MTF working side-by-side - nobody's exchanging uniforms.

That isn't to say what's mine has to be yours. I'm upvoting this, because it's coherent, well-written and tells an interesting story. It's obvious that an enormous amount of effort has been dedicated towards it, and that's always commendable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The core's good, but it is just absolutely buried in unnecessary words, even before you get to the tabs and footnotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Adding my voice to this. Far too much here (I've never seen a more superfluous footnote than #7, possibly also #4) drowning out the good stuff, and overall I'm not left with much impression beyond "cool use of mythology".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To build on this, my main problem with the article was how much of the Sarkic content felt like a rehash of the Sarkicism Hub or previous articles about Sarkicism: the Rite of Nyúz, akuloths, the Esoteric Order of the White Worm, the Solomonari, N,N-DMT, Sitra Achra, etc. In my opinion, while there's nothing intrinsically wrong with these references, their sheer volume drowns out your article's original elements. (I quite enjoyed the cosmology that you described, and the exploration of more benign forms of Sarkicism.) In light of this, I think that the article could be improved if you reduced its length and refocused it on your original concepts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This pretty much succinctly captures my view on the article. I much preferred the GoI-format to this, simply because of its relative succinctness and the fact that unlike Sarkicism, Manna Charitable is something that I'm not completely sick of. From what I read before losing interest entirely, this doesn't do enough to play Sarkicism in a way that hasn't been done (multiple times) before by either Metaphysician's original SCPs or other authors who have used Sarkicism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: IMO, way too many footnotes. Scale back on them, maybe review them and see what you could do without.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've written a lot of long articles, and the real challenge with long-form articles is maintaining interest throughout. This doesn't do that for me. The deluge of information turns it into a slog.

Additionally, and this is just a personal gripe, but I'm more than worn out on Sarkicism. I just don't find it interesting anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only real complaint is that it's too long. You have three collapsables, five tabs, and twenty footnotes! Consider splitting out the exploration log into its own page, at least.

This actually might work really well as three pages: the main SCP article, which teases but doesn't reveal much; a supplement about the villagers and their culture; and the exploration log and epilogue. The Manna document is either a fourth page or merged into the log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The come-down might what's causing those odd sensations.

Small typo here. Also, the last part of the exploration log, which should be italicized, isn't working. You'll need to put the // at the end of each paragraph.

Besides from that, this was a really cool scip. The Sarkic flag was also a nice touch, which I can see being used a lot in the future (because vexillology is great).

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is now one of my favourite Karcist skips, nice work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No problem. Something about knowing being half the battle. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I'll be sure to keep that in mind for future uploads.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please don't upload massive images (in your case approx. 7 MB) to your article. The image component will only display them at 300 px wide, but the bandwidth use for mobile viewers is still that 7 MB. I've downsized (and reduced the dpi to 72) and reuploaded them for you, but please make sure your images are suitable for use on the web before you post.

item.title: Re: Problems
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I'll definitely keep working on this, though it might take some time. (Right now, it's 3:30 AM where I live, so I'll be needing some sleep first.)

item.title: Thanks to:
item.content:encoded:
I would like to extend a heartfelt thanks to all the people who've helped me craft this article through their feedback and ideas. There has been a ton of things that I've had to change, but it did make it a better article in the end.

So I would like to thank:

  • Metaphysician
  • Rimple
  • minmin
  • Shaggydredlocks
  • Flappybobb
  • LadyKatie
  • Cimmerian

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah! Thanks for pointing that out! Fixed it now.

And thanks! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Incredible effort. Some parts of this story scared me shitless. Anyway, the special containment procedures section doesn't require an extra line after it, backspace it a bit until it looks like this:

Special Containment Procedures: SCP-2815 is to be monitored on a 24-hour basis

Overall, good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
EDIT: Forgot to credit the image author.

IMAGE SOURCE
Site: Wikimedia Commons
Author: Rakás
Licence: Creative-Commons
Date: 05/02/2016

Site: DeviantArt
Author: SunnyClockwork
Licence: Creative-Commons
Date: 03/09/2015

Wow, you guys are quick. I haven't even announced this yet. XD

A few of the XXXX's escaped my attention during the initial posting. I hope to have corrected them by this point.

Yeah, about the rating module: how do I put it there? I thought it was automatically assigned to each tale and article.

Been rereading this several times, trying to fix spelling and grammatical errors, so might have turned blind to them. If you notice any that I've missed, feel free to correct them if you have the time. It'd be much appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's alright- an interbreeding pagan cult. It's well written, but all those ritual defails should be on another page.

item.title: Problems
item.content:encoded:
Heres a generic list (not complete in any means)

1. No rating module

2. Needs to be edited, a lot of gramma, punctuation and spelling errors.

3. SCP-XXXX crops up in a few places.

Idea is cool but thats about it, the rest of the article needs work.

EDIT: Holy you have a lot of content, but its presented in a way that doesn't make me want to read it all. I'm sure you can salvage some gems from this

EDIT 2: After another rereading i regress, this idea is really cool and dramatic. I'll sit a bit more before deciding absolutely so no vote for now but you can count on my +1 if it receives more polish :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't get me wrong, this looks cool, but you forgot the rating model and I see some SCP-XXXX's here and there.
